coredata.dc:title: Utility of cauliflower waste/garlic biomass admixture for green synthesis of multi-doped CQDs as a fluorescent optosensor for Fe3+ in different matrices

coredata.dc:creator: Hesham, Raneem
Abd El-Aziz, Heba
El-Shaheny, Rania
coredata.dc:description:
A simple method for the synthesis of multi-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been developed via a fast, affordable, and green pathway from natural, nontoxic, and inexpensive substrates as garlic and cauliflower leaves and stalk as a plant by-product by the aid of microwave. The synthesized fluorescent CQDs have excellent quantum yield of 42 % at λex/λem of 330/411 nm with superb stability. The CQDs were fully described using energy-dispersive X-ray, X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Zeta potential, UV–visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy which demonstrate that CQDs are doped with multiple elements including sulfur, nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, sodium, and calcium. It has been noted that Fe3
+ specifically quenches the CQDs’ fluorescence, unlike other heavy metals. Based on this phenomenon, the produced CQDs revealed outstanding results as a fluorescent optosensor for iron determination over the concentration range of 10.0–400.0 μM with a limit of detection of 3.29 μM. The proposed method is effective for determining Fe3+ in pharmaceutical dosage form with mean % recovery of 98.73 ± 0.75 %. Additionally, the synthesized CQDs also showed a significant promising potential in environmental applications for detection of Fe3
+ in tap, irrigation, and river water with mean % recovery of 99.45 ± 1.71, 100.57 ± 1.32, and 101.49 ± 2.44 %, respectively. Several greenness measures and software approved the greenness of the designed optosensor. This approach proved that green synthesis could furnish CQDs from natural low-cost substrates with distinct outstanding characteristics that are suited for certain uses without consuming hazard materials unlike traditional methods.

(a) TEM images and the inset shows the HRTEM images of a single CQD, (b) histogram for particle size distribution, (c) SAED pattern (hexagon lines were drawn to highlight the pattern), and (d) X-ray diffraction pattern, (e) FT-IR spectroscopy, (f) Zeta potential distribution, and (g) EDX spectrum of the prepared CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of CQDs and UV absorptions spectra of CQDs and Fe+3 (b) Excitation-dependent emission of CQDs using λex 300–400 nm, (c) Effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs (error bars represents SD of three determinations), (d) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different pH values, and (e) Thermal stability of CQDs at 25–80 °C.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (10.0–400.0 μM) and the inset represents the calibration curve of Fe3+ (b) % suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected fluorescence of CQDs after addition of several concentrations of Fe3+, (c) the Stern-Volmer's study of CQDs-Fe3+ system at different temperatures, and (d) schematic representation of the mechanism of CQDs’ fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ via IFE and ground state complex formation.

head.ce:title: Utility of cauliflower waste/garlic biomass admixture for green synthesis of multi-doped CQDs as a fluorescent optosensor for Fe3+ in different matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A simple method for the synthesis of multi-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been developed via a fast, affordable, and green pathway from natural, nontoxic, and inexpensive substrates as garlic and cauliflower leaves and stalk as a plant by-product by the aid of microwave. The synthesized fluorescent CQDs have excellent quantum yield of 42 % at λex/λem of 330/411 nm with superb stability. The CQDs were fully described using energy-dispersive X-ray, X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Zeta potential, UV–visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy which demonstrate that CQDs are doped with multiple elements including sulfur, nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, sodium, and calcium. It has been noted that Fe3
+ specifically quenches the CQDs’ fluorescence, unlike other heavy metals. Based on this phenomenon, the produced CQDs revealed outstanding results as a fluorescent optosensor for iron determination over the concentration range of 10.0–400.0 μM with a limit of detection of 3.29 μM. The proposed method is effective for determining Fe3+ in pharmaceutical dosage form with mean % recovery of 98.73 ± 0.75 %. Additionally, the synthesized CQDs also showed a significant promising potential in environmental applications for detection of Fe3
+ in tap, irrigation, and river water with mean % recovery of 99.45 ± 1.71, 100.57 ± 1.32, and 101.49 ± 2.44 %, respectively. Several greenness measures and software approved the greenness of the designed optosensor. This approach proved that green synthesis could furnish CQDs from natural low-cost substrates with distinct outstanding characteristics that are suited for certain uses without consuming hazard materials unlike traditional methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are the core of an emerging technological revolution as they have distinct chemical and physical characteristics endowing multiple applications [1–4]. For example, fluorescent nanoparticles exhibit favorable chemical robustness, photostability, and optical characteristics that can be controlled. Fluorescent nanoparticles are frequently employed as fluorescent sensors for a variety of purposes due to their design and functionality [5,6]. There are different types of nanomaterials such as carbon-based nanomaterials, metal-based nanomaterials (silver, gold, copper, …..), dendrimers, and composites [5]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a lot of interest because they are not only overcoming the drawbacks of conventional organic dyes and labelling agents, but they are also superior to metal nanoparticles, in terms of tiny particle size, low toxicity, biodegradability, ease of functionalization, variety of substrate sources, affordability, high fluorescence, and eco-friendliness [7].

There are two approaches for the synthesis of nanomaterials: (i) top-down approach that is done by converting large material into tiny and nanoscale particles and (ii) down-top approach, this method is the opposite of top-down as nanomaterials are formed through the growth and self-assembly of atoms and molecules as their building blocks. There are many methods for synthesizing nanomaterials embracing chemical, hydrothermal, and physical methods [8] but these methods have some downsides as they are too complex and time-consuming, they probably need specific instrumentations and require a lot of energy to generate nanomaterials. Besides, these methods contradict the approach of green chemistry that emerged as one of the newest and most rapidly growing branches of chemistry, aiming to inoculate the concept of sustainability into laboratory culture for developing inexpensive and safe analytical procedures through simple processes, natural resources, and toxic chemical-free procedures. The use of biomass renewable organic materials that derive from plants, animals, or microorganisms as a feedstock for generating CQDs gained much interest by virtue of their availability, cost-efficiency, and suitability for execution in different laboratories [3,8–14].

Hence, in this study we implemented a green, cheap, sustainable, and energy/time efficient technique for the synthesis of CQDs using a widely accessible domestic microwave for eight minutes at a low power (700 W) and available plant biomass. We have chosen garlic cloves (Allium sativum) and cauliflower (Brassica oleracea var. botrytis) by-produced leaves and stalk as widely available and low-price natural biomass to synthesize excellent CQDs. The developed technique avoids the need for special practices such as mechanical milling, nanolithography, laser ablation, sputtering, or electron explosion, that is a main drawback of top-down approach for the synthesis of nanomaterials.

Garlic is a source of sulfur as it contains about 33 sulfur compounds beside vitamins (A, B1, B2, B6, C), minerals (K, Na, Ca, Mg, ….), polyphenols, nicotinic acid, phospholipids, and fatty acids [9]. Whereas cauliflower is a widely used vegetable either on an industrial or household scale, while its leaves and stalk are generally thrown away as a valueless food waste, despite they are the richest part of the plant in bioactive compounds such as crude protein, carotene, lysine, tryptophan, and natural antioxidants including phenols [15,16]. Thus, the rational of the present study is based on mixing these two natural biomasses to induce CQDs synthesis and self-doping with heteroatoms. Cauliflower by-products serve as the main carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen source, while garlic cloves are the major source of heteroatoms dopants like sulfur, phosphorus, and minerals, beside carbon and oxygen. The heteroatoms in the biomass concurrently afford doping for CQDs. Heteroatom doping has a great potential to enhance the luminescence efficiency, water solubility, and stability of the CQDs [17].The furnished CQDs have high fluorescence, that allowed their application as an effective fluorescence optosensor for pharmaceutical and environmental analysis. So, the synthesized CQDs have been utilized as optosensor for determination and detection of Fe3+ in pharmaceutical and water samples.

Iron is one of the many minerals needed by the human body and it is a vital component of the human diet. It is crucial for several biological functions, including respiration, RNA and DNA synthesis, gene expression regulation, and electron transport [18]. It is a necessary component in the production of the oxygen-carrying proteins myoglobin and haemoglobin. In addition, iron is needed for other vital processes, such as the production of strong immunological responses, mental and cell growth. People often utilize iron to cure and prevent various forms of anaemia brought on by low iron levels. Along with many other ailments, it is also utilized for heart failure, ADHD, child development, memory and thinking problems, and exhaustion [19]. Though, high concentrations of iron have problems on human body, water, and environment. Iron overloaded on human body is characterized by endocrine dysfunction, cell death, fibrosis, and carcinogenesis in addition to the failure of essential organs like the liver and heart [20]. The high iron content in water imparts a metallic flavor and smell. Moreover, water may seem turbid and contains particles [21]. Thus, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) stated the maximum iron in water to be 0.3 mg/L [22]. However, higher levels of iron in environmental water result from industrial activities such as mining, manufacturing units, and industrial wastewater beside municipal wastewater. Moreover, high level of iron arises from pipes corrosion, groundwater supply, and from the atmosphere via rainwater. The pollution of water with Fe3+ leads to accumulation of Fe3+ in the food chain and harmful impact on the aquatic organisms. Besides, the high level of Fe3+ in water has detrimental effect on human and lead to multiple organs injury [23]. Elevated iron levels may act as a growing medium for certain bacteria, such as E. coli. Additionally, increasing iron concentration leads to the creation of free radicals, which harm proteins, DNA, and membranes as well as the structure of plant cells. In certain plants, iron toxicity is linked to decreased photosynthesis and yield as well as elevated oxidative stress [24]. Thus, there is a persistent need for a fast, green, simple, and accurate method for both quality controlling of Fe3
+ -containing pharmaceutical formulations and inspecting its level in different environmental water samples.

A variety of techniques have been widely employed to determine Fe3+, including spectrophotometric [25,26], ion chromatography [27], electrochemical method [28], HPLC [29], and spectrofluorimetric [30] methods. Most of these techniques lack sensitivity, need exhausting work, wasting time, require costly equipment, trained operators and their use of dangerous chemicals or organic solvents. Hence, it is important to develop a new simple, sensitive, and rapid method for iron determination. Spectrofluorimetric-based methods have remarkable merits due to high sensitivity, simple operation, and fast analysis [31–34]. Thus, in this study, the synthesis of multi-doped CQDs from natural biomass by using microwave has been implemented, which is easier, faster, greener, more affordable, environmentally friendly, and less toxic compared to the previous methods. The synthesized multi-doped CQDs were used for quantitative determination of iron where the inner filter effect exerted by iron as well as the formation of a non-fluorescent Fe3+-CQDs complex cause fluorescence of CQDs to quench with increasing Fe3
+ concentration. The designed optosensor has been used for water analysis and applied to different environmental water samples. As well, it has been applied for pharmaceutical analysis of Fe3+-containing dosage forms.
Instruments utilized in this study are described in the Supporting Material” Section “S1.1. Instruments” .

Chemicals and solvents utilized were of high analytical grade. Sodium hydroxide, cobalt chloride, mercuric chloride, copper chloride, nickel chloride, quinine sulphate, and boric acid were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). Phosphoric acid and acetic acid were obtained from Adwic Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Ferric chloride, cadmium sulphate, and lead acetate were supplied by Piochem Co. (Cairo, Egypt) Sacrofer® ampoules (Amoun Co., Cairo, Egypt) labelled to contain 100 mg elemental iron/5mL (as iron saccharate 285 mg) were acquired from a nearby pharmacy.

A stock solution of FeCl3 was prepared in distilled water and diluted with same solvent to obtain 1 mM FeCl3 standard solution. The pH of an aqueous solution containing 0.04 M acetic acid, 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and 0.04 M boric acid was modified with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to prepare Britton Robinson buffer (BRB).

In this study, we applied a fast, eco-friendly, and affordable method for preparation of CQDs. Cauliflower and Garlic were purchased from a nearby grocery in Mansoura, Egypt. The garlic's outer skin was peeled off, the cloves were washed with tap water then distilled water, dried at room temperature, and chopped into tiny fragments. Home grinder was used to grind these little pieces of garlic. In the same manner, cauliflower's leaves and stalk were washed with tap and distilled water, dried at room temperature, and roughly cut into little fragments. By using a home grinder, the fragments were crushed to a fine consistency. The resultant cauliflower waste and garlic fine pastes were combined in a specific proportion. Several experiments were conducted to find the best ratio of garlic and cauliflower waste pastes to optimize the synthesis process. It was found that the ideal ratio of garlic and cauliflower waste is 7.5:2.5 (g/g) which gives the highest fluorescence intensity. The pastes were accurately weighed at this ratio and transferred to 250 mL beaker, thoroughly mixed, and put in the microwave at 700 W for 8 min. The resultant black residue was scratched with a spatula, 20 mL distilled water has been added, then sonicated for 20 min followed by filtration to obtain pure filtrate. The filtrate was then centrifuged for 30 min at 6000 rpm. A 0.45 μm filtration syringe was used to filter the supernatant. The resulting brown solution was kept in an amber-coloured container for later usage. The bottle was kept in a refrigerator at 4°C to remain stable for more than six months. Working solution of CQDs was prepared by diluting 0.1 mL of the prepared solution to 10 mL with distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield calculation of the synthesized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Calculation of the relative quantum yield (ΦΧ ) of the generated CQDs has been performed according to the reported literature [35] and described in the Supporting Material, Section “S1.2. Fluorescence quantum yield calculation of the synthesized CQDs”.

Accurately measured volumes of 500 μL of CQDs working solution has been added to a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks after which different series of volumes of standard solution of iron were added. A volume of 1500 μL of BRB of pH 3.0 was added to each flask followed by distilled water to complete to the mark then shake well. The fluorescence (F) was measured at 411 nm after excitation at 330 nm. Blank experiment was carried out by eliminating iron and the fluorescence of blank was recorded (F0). Each experiment was repeated three times. Plotting Ln (F0/F) against iron concentration [μM] led to the construction of the calibration curve, and the regression equation was then obtained.

The contents of 5 ampoules (Sacrofer®) have been mixed and precisely measured and a volume equivalent to 0.027 g Fe3+ was transferred into 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to the final volume with distilled water to obtain 1 mM of iron solution. Various quantities of iron have been added to series of 10 mL volumetric flasks containing 500 μL of CQDs working solution and 1500 μL of BRB (pH 3.0). The produced solutions were then handled as indicated in section “2.5.1” to calculate the concentration of iron. The %recovery and standard deviation were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of iron in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To test the practicality of the method, the prepared CQDs were used to evaluate the presence of Fe3
+ in water samples. Tap water was obtained from our laboratory, irrigation water was obtained from a canal in Mit-Antar village, Dakahlia, Egypt, and river water sample was obtained from Nile River branch, Mansoura, Egypt. All water samples were filtered with 0.45 μm filtration syringe. A volume of 3 mL of each water sample was transferred to set of 10 mL volumetric flasks containing 500 μL of CQDs working solution and 1500 μL of BRB (pH 3.0) the method outlined in section 2.5.1 was applied to detect Fe3
+ . Blank experiment was conducted using distilled water instead of environmental water. Each experiment was done in triplicate. For Fe3+ spiking experiments, each type of water sample has been spiked with increasing concentration of Fe3+ (10.0, 40.0, and 80.0 µM). The same procedure described earlier in section 2.5.1 has been done and % recovery has been calculated for each sample.

A fast, easy, affordable, and green method has been optimized for preparing CQDs. The developed synthesis method is a superior choice for the preparation of CQDs compared to the reported synthesis methods that suffers from some drawbacks like consumption of energy, time, chemicals, and materials. In this study, we apply a green method for CQDs synthesis from two natural, widespread, and cheap substrates: garlic and cauliflower by-product (leaves and stalk) by using a home microwave. Previous research showed that the use of dry garlic powder alone as a substrate for CQDs synthesis via hydrothermal route (200 °C, 6 h) resulted in poor quantum yield (maximum 10 %). This necessitates the addition of an external synthetic nitrogen source like ethylenediamine to improve the quantum yield to be 20 % [36].Yet, the addition of a chemical substrate contradicts the sustainability of the synthesis. This inspired us to formulate a sustainable biomass admixture by combining garlic with a nitrogen-rich leafy green like cauliflower by-products (leaves and stalk) that contain more than 17 amino acids in copious amounts [35]. This approach was very successful since it furnished CQDs with a superior quantum yield of 42 %.

It has been investigated how the ratio of garlic to cauliflower by-product (10:0, 7.5:2.5, 5:5, 2.5:7.5, and 0:10 g/g, respectively) affects the fluorescence intensity, while remaining the microwave irradiation time unchanged as shown in Fig. 1 a. Analysing the obtained experimental results revealed that two factors have a key influence on the synthesis process: the precursors ratio and the medium homogeneity. This can be explained as follows:
(i)
Using either garlic or cauliflower by-products alone resulted in remarkably lower fluorescence than when mixed in 7.5:2.5 or 2.5:7.5 g/g, respectively. This manifests the importance of the existence of a combination of the two admixture components as synthesis precursors since either component alone yield weaker fluorescence.
(ii)
It is observed that, when garlic and cauliflower by-products were mixed in 5:5, g/g ratio, the obtained CQDs showed lower fluorescence intensity than those obtained using 7.5:2.5 or 2.5:7.5 g/g. This is probably attributed to the better medium homogeneity as one component exists in excess than the other. The presence of one component greater than the other allows more homogenous environment and substrates density suitable for initiation of carbonization and microwave radiation transfer through the medium.
(iii)
The weaker fluorescence given by a mixture of garlic and cauliflower by-products (5:5, g/g, respectively) than that of 10:0 and 0:10, g/g mixtures despite containing the two precursors is attributed to the barely homogenous medium that may hinders the carbonization and the synthesis process.
Thus, as a compromise between the medium homogeneity and the substrates ratio, we utilized 7.5:2.5 g/g of garlic: cauliflower by-product as the optimum synthesis precursors.

Furthermore, the microwave irradiation time has been studied from 4 to 12 min using 7.5:2.5 g/g of garlic: cauliflower by-product and 8 min is the optimum irradiation microwave time as illustrated in Fig. 1b. Further increase of the microwave irradiation time led to a decrease of the quantum yield. The explanation of this behavior has been reported by Bagheri et al. [37] who suggested two possible reasons: (i) the formation of larger carbon particles after long heating times which wanes the overall fluorescence emission and/or (ii) the possibility of increasing the degree of carbonization up on longer microwave irradiation that may result in formation of side products that diminished the fluorescence brightness. A diagram showing CQDs synthesis processes is provided in Fig. 1c.

The size and shape of the prepared CQDs had been determined by TEM imaging. Fig. 2 a shows the presence of monodispersed CQDs with nearly spherical shape and average size of 5.5 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the CQDs (Inset of Fig. 2a) showed that CQDs particles displayed well-defined fringes which confirmed their crystallinity. The average d-spacing calculated from the HRTEM is 0.28 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [38]. Besides, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs (Fig. 2c) reveals a matched symmetry hexagonal crystalline structure [39]. The weak appearance of the diffused rings in the SAED pattern is probably attributed to disruption of the CQDs by the electron radiation beam [40]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to inspect the crystallinity of the synthesized CQDs. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 2d) approves the crystalline nature of the CQDs with a broad peak around 2θ = 25° because of the amorphous carbonaceous core–shell material [41]. The sharp peaks at 28.5°, 37.7°, 44°, and 64.4° correspond to the (0 0 2), (0 0 2), (1 0 0), and (1 0 3) crystal planes of graphite (sp2) carbon [42–46] and the peak at 77.3° corresponds to (2 2 0) crystal plane that signifies diamond like sp3 carbon [47]. The XRD results showed a very good agreement with the results of HRTEM and SAED.

Chemical functionality has been investigated by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. This offers important details regarding functional groups and chemical bonds that present in the CQDs. As seen in Fig. 2e, there is a broad peak at 3441 cm−1 refers to O-H or N-H stretching vibration band and weak peak at 2087 cm−1 belongs to C≡C stretching vibration. Narrow peak at 1639 cm−1 is specified to C=N or C=C stretching or N-H bending vibration. Vibration with S=O or S=C stretching, or P-O-alkyl bending is responsible for the peak at 1125 cm−1, while 1040 cm−1 responsible S=O stretch or P-O-aryl bend. Vibrational band at 949 cm−1 is attributed to C=C stretch, S=N stretch, O-H bend, or P-O-C bend. Furthermore, stretching vibration of S-S is the explanation for the peak at 662 cm−1 [48]. FTIR data validated the presence of different functional groups, incorporating nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, and oxygen, on CQDs' surfaces which improve aqueous solubility. The charge on the surface of CQDs has been assessed using zeta potential (ζ). Zeta potential, as seen in Fig. 2f, is equal to –22.1 mv. The strong negative value of ζ offers good stability to the CQDs because the highly negatively charged CQDs repel each other and stop them from aggregating.

Furthermore, elemental composition was investigated using energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) analysis to investigate the elements present in the prepared CQDs and their concentration. The results of this study (Fig. 2g) showed that the furnished CQDs are composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorus, and minor amounts of sodium, calcium, potassium, and chloride.

The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) corroborate the CQDs' surface chemical composition. XPS survey scan spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 3 a) reveals that they consist of carbon (67.14 %), oxygen (24.41 %), nitrogen (2.86 %), sulphur (0.3 %) and phosphorous (1.32 %), in addition to traces of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride (not exceeding 3.9 % in total). The main peaks are found to be carbon (285.06 eV), nitrogen (399.77 eV), sulphur (164.2 eV), and oxygen (531.96 eV). This suggests that sulphur, phosphorous, and nitrogen have been successfully doped into the CQDs. According to Fig. 3b, the high-resolution spectra of C1s shows three peaks at 284.52, 286.01, and 287.86 eV, which correspond to the following: C-C/C=C, C-O/C-S/C-N, and C=O, respectively[49]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the high-resolution spectrum of S2p shows three distinct peaks at 162.74, 166.24, and 168.96 eV, that are related to S2−, SO2, and SO3 2−/SO4 2−, respectively [50,51]. In the high-resolution spectra of N1s, the two peaks at 399.49 and 399.6 eV correspond to C=N/C-N, and C-NR2(R = C, H) [52,53] respectively (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e illustrates that the high-resolution spectrum of O1s was deconvoluted into two peaks, that indicate metal oxide and C=O, respectively, at 530.73 and 532.19 eV [49,50]. The two peaks at 132.85 and 133.9 eV were identified in the high-resolution spectrum of P2p which correspond to PO3 3−and PO4 3−, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3f.[50].

An insight into the reported elemental composition of the biomass utilized as a feedstock in the synthesis of CQDs by the present method explains the sources of the co-dopants. Garlic is rich in potassium (48.1 %), phosphorus (18.3 %), sulphur (17.2 %), calcium (10.7 %), and sodium (1.3 %) [54,55]. On the other hand, the highest antioxidant power and the greatest phytochemical content of cauliflower are in the leaves and stalk, which to date are considered a by-product of cauliflower processing. The leaves and stalk of cauliflower are rich in proteins (mainly glutamate amino acid), fats, carbohydrates, glucosinolates, and phenolics [56,57]. From these facts, we can conclude that the main carbon and nitrogen source is cauliflower leaves and stalk, while garlic is the main source of sulphur, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, and sodium.

The prepared CQDS revealed two absorption maxima in the UV area at about 210 and 285 nm, as seen in Fig. 4 a. The absorption maximum at 210 nm is ascribed for the π-π* transition of C=C of the heterocyclic core of CQDs. However, the near UV-band at 285 nm is caused by the n–π* transition of carbonyl/amine groups [58]. As observed in Fig. 4a, after CQDs are excited at 330 nm, they show an intense fluorescence at 411 nm. From Fig. 4b, it is evident that the CQDs showed excitation-dependent emission. When the excitation wavelength was altered from 300 to 340 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was nearly constant, even while the FI grew significantly up to 340 nm. The maximum emission wavelength red shifts and the FI progressively drops as the wavelength of excitation is raised from 350 to 400 nm. This excitation dependent emission behaviour belongs to the surface states similar to what has previously seen in the literature [59].

At λex/λem of 330/411 nm, the maximum FI was obtained. The CQDs shown superior fluorescence qualities when compared to CQDs made from various sources [14,60–71], with a relative quantum yield of 42 % utilizing quinine sulphate as a standard. The unique raw material for synthesis of CQDs offered multiple heteroatoms doping with nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorous which is the main factor for enhancing the fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized CQDs. Besides, minor dopants like sodium and potassium introduce Na-O/K-O interrelated defect luminescent center in the band gap resulting in blue fluorescence with high quantum yield [72]. The heteroatoms doping alters the interior structure or surface state of CQDs, thus modifying their energy gap width and electron density, which is favorable to upgrade their quantum yield. Furthermore, heteroatom dopants can capture exciton leading to surface state-linked fluorescence. The different functional groups detected on the synthesized CQDs surface have miscellaneous structural configurations and variable energy states causing diverse recombination options of electrons and holes captured by surface states. These attributes corroborate the high quantum yield of the prepared CQDs [73].

One essential factor for employing CQDs as a fluorescence sensor is their stability. The pH impact on CQDs fluorescence was measured by using BRB throughout a pH range from 2.0 to 12.0 (n = 3). Fig. 4c reveals that a drop in FI is only seen when the pH was lower than 3.0 or more than 8.0 while a constant and a stable FI is attained over the pH range of 3.0–8.0 indicating good stability of the CQDs. At extremely acidic pH (< 3.0), the CQDs aggregated since the –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of CQDs tend to be undissociated which inhibit repulsion between CQDs. Besides, protonation of the surface amino groups at highly acidic pH leads to increasing the capacity of intramolecular electron transference between –NH3 + and CQDs resulting in decreasing in their fluorescence. However, –COOH and –OH groups deprotonate when the pH is within the range of 3.0–8.0 and exist as –COO− and –O−. This increases the negative charges on the surface and stops the CQDs from aggregating, which improves their fluorescence. Conversely, at pH more than 8.0, it is more likely that electrons emitted via amino group lone pair will pass towards the CQDs, suppressing the fluorescence [74,75]. Though, the peak position was nearly unchanged from pH 2.0–12.0 (Fig. 4d) which is a reported behaviour in previous researches [76,77].

In addition, the CQDs remained stable when stored at 4 °C for almost six months, exhibiting unchanged fluorescence with no noticeable sedimentation or deposition. Furthermore, the stability of the synthesized CQDs was investigated across a broad heating temperature range (25–80 °C). The results showed that the CQDs demonstrate excellent thermal stability, which is proved by the fact that the emission intensity almost remains constant after the heating process as shown in Fig. 4e. Such findings indicate that CQDs have exceptional stability, making it a promising option for many applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Positioning the developed method for synthesis of CQDs among the literature
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested microwave synthesis approach is superior to previously documented synthesis methods because the synthesis method and the carbon source are more environmentally friendly, and synthesized from inexpensive, widely accessible plants instead of chemicals or organic solvents. As shown in Table S1, the produced CQDs have a higher quantum yield than the systems that have been described [14,60–71]. This method is 45–360 times faster than hydrothermal methods [60,62–64,66,68,69]. Furthermore, the synthesis of the present CQDs consumes much less energy than reported hydrothermally synthesized CQDs which mandate lengthy heating at high temperature (120–200 °C) for 3–48 hr [60,62–64,66,68,69]. Additionally, this method is superior to other microwave synthesis methods [14,61,65,67,70,71] in terms of quantum yield.
Thus, the synthesized CQDs show promising capabilities due to their high quantum yield, outstanding stability, and rapid synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Iron determination using CQDs as a sensor: Principle and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Upon addition of increasing concentrations of iron ranging from (10.0–400.0 μM) to the CQDs, the high fluorescence of CQDs gradually decreased (Fig. 5 a). The results showed that, in the presence of 400.0 μM Fe3+, nearly 75 % of the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched. Various procedures have been applied to identify the quenching mechanism. First, experiment has been conducted for identifying the potential overlaps between the iron absorption spectrum and CQDs excitation and emission spectra, that may cause the primary and secondary inner filter effects (IFE), respectively [78]. IFE correction was carried out by applying Eq. (2) to determine the corrected FI after the elimination of IFE [78]:
(2)
F
corrected
=
F
observed
× a n t i l o g
Aex + A e m
2
Fcorrected is FI corrected after elimination of IFE.
Fobserved is FI observed.
Aex and Aem are Fe3+ absorbance at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the CQDs, respectively.

Following this, the % suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for the corrected and observed FI's using Eq. (3).
(3)
% E = [ 1 -
F
F
0
] × 100

A significant reduction (≈90 %) of the suppression efficiency resulted from IFE subtraction. The results shown in Fig. 5b verified that, IFE is the main reason for quenching of fluorescence of CQDs by Fe3+ .

Further evidence of the quenching mechanism has been supplied by applying Stern-Volmer equationof the quenching of fluorescence at rising temperature levels (298, 323, and 343 K) in accordance with Eq. (4) [70].
(4)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ F
e
3 +
]
KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.
[Fe3+ ] is the quencher molar concentration.

When molecules make an association complex in the ground state, this is known as a static quenching. In this case, KSV reduced with increasing temperature while dynamic quenching happens due to collision of fluorophore in the state of excitation with the quencher that lead to disabling the fluorophore to the ground state. According to dynamic state, by increasing temperature, the KSV increases [79]. Conversely, IFE is temperature-independent, and it is not a form of static or dynamic quenching. A plot of F0∕F versus [Fe3
+ ] at different temperature settings is displayed in Fig. 5c, from which the slope (KSV) is calculated at each temperature. As can be seen from the data in the inset table of Fig. 5c, the calculated KSV values are temperature-dependent where they decrease in a stepwise manner with the increase in temperature indicating static quenching that take place via complexation between Fe3
+ and the CQDs. The carboxylic, amino, hydroxyl, phenolic, and phosphate surface functionalities of the CQDs prove strong interaction between Fe3
+ and the CQDs, consequently supporting the formation of a complex and improving fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5d). The fluorescence quenching of the CQDs can be accredited to the successive electron transfer from CQDs surface to Fe3+ resulting in fluorescence quenching [80]. Hence, it is concluded that a combination of IFE and static quenching are responsible for the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by Fe3
+ .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the analytical parameters for the determination of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of various experimental parameters, such as pH, buffer volume, and CQDs volume on the CQDs' fluorescence quenching has been tested to investigate the ideal experimental parameters and promote the accurate detectionof iron. The impact of pH has been examined using BRBof pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, and 8.0 to investigate the potential interference of metal ions like Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Co2+, Mg2+, and Cd2+. It has been determined that pH 3.0 is ideal for selective determination of Fe3+ without any interference of the tested metals (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). On the other hand, at pH 5.0, 7.0, and 8.0, Hg2+, Ni2+, and Cu2+ interfere with the determination of Fe3+ to different extents (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). It is worth noting that the predominating iron species at the working pH (pH 3.0) is Fe3+. On the other hand, Fe(OH)2 + exists at pH 4.0–8.0, while at pH>8.0, Fe(OH)4 − predominates. Thus, uncertainty in sampling process is minimal [81].

By adjusting the buffer's volume over the range of 500–2000 μL, the impact of the volume has been investigated. The quenching efficiency remained relatively unaffected by the buffer volume, as Fig. S2a illustrates. The ideal volume of BRB of pH 3.0 has been determined to be 1500 μL. A study of the impact of the CQDs volume (200–800 μL) on the efficacy of the method has also been conducted. 500 μL was found to yield the best quenching efficiency as shown in Fig. S2b, so this volume was chosen as the ideal one. Incubation time has been studied over the range of 0 to 45 min. It has been found that the quenching effect of Fe3+ takes place immediately and further incubation has no influence on the quenching efficiency as illustrated in Fig. S2c.
Thus, the optimized determination of Fe3+ is conducted at pH 3.0 utilizing BRB (1500 μL) and 500 μL of the prepared CQDs via measurement of the FI at λex/λem of 330/411 nm.

In the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs has been examined (Fig. 5a). The Ln (F0 /F) readings matched the provided equation for regression and showed a significant linear relation with the concentration of Fe3+ within the range of 10.0–400.0 μM:
(5)
L n
F 0
/ F
= 0.106 x - 0.002
r = 0.9999
x is Fe3+ concentration [μM]
r is correlation coefficient.

The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were computed according to ICH Guidelines [82] as 3.3σ/S and 10σ/S, respectively (the standard deviation of the response is represented by σ, and the slope of the calibration curve by S). It has been found that 3.29 and 9.99 μM are the respective values for LOD and LOQ.

Also, assessment of the method accuracy and precision has been conducted both within and between days [82]. By measuring the concentration of Fe3
+ (10.0, 150.0, and 350.0 μM) three times in a single day, the method's intra-day precision has been evaluated. Inter-day precision was done by determining the same three concentrations of Fe3+ for three successive days. The great precision of the developed approach is proved by the obtained results of the relative standard deviation (RSD) and standard deviation (SD) which are less than 1.7 % (Table S2).

Applying the Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [83], the results of the Fe3+ determination by the proposed method were assessed against the findings of a comparison technique [84].The computed t- and F-values [83] are smaller than the theoretical values, as indicated by Table 2, suggesting that there is no discernible difference between the two methods' performances, which confirm its accuracy and precision, respectively.

The influence of various potentially co-existing metal ions, such as Cu2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, and K+, and anions such as Cl−, Br−, and SO4 2− has been investigated under detection conditions to ascertain their effect on the fluorescence response in order to assess the selectivity of the prepared CQDs for Fe3+ in the presence of other species. The findings shown in Table1
demonstrate the selectivity of the method since the exsistance of such metal ions and anions shows a negligible impact on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs where the %change in the FI was with in a very narrow range (−0.3 to + 0.15 %). This study validates the method for selective determination of Fe3+ in different matrices without any interference from these species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the CQDs as an optosensor for Fe3+
Determination of Fe3+ in commercial dosage form
ce:section.ce:para: The method's accuracy in determining Fe3+ in its commercially available ampoules was assessed for real application. Data in Table 2 represents the high %found (98.20–99.60 %) and small values of SD (± 0.75) which proves the efficacy and potentiality of the proposed method for estimation and quality control of Fe3+ in dosage form without interference from common additives. The results of the developed optosensor have been compared with the results obtained using the comparison methd [84] by student t-test and variance ratio F-test showing no significant difference regarding accuracy and precision, respectively [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in various environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
It is particularly essential to efficiently inspect the level of Fe3+ in water to prevent the dangerous influences of high Fe3+ level on biological health [23]. Therefore, the developed CQDs optosensor has been applied for the determination of Fe3
+ in different samples of environmental water obtained from different sources including tap, irrigation, and Nile River water. Experiments showed that all the screeened samples do not contain iron or even the concentration of Fe3+ in these water samples might be below the LOD of the developed method (3.29 μM). Hence, spiking experiments were conducted to examine the recovery and performance of the method in these samples. As shown in Table 3 , mean % recoveries ± SD were 99.45 % ±1.71, 100.57 % ±1.32, and 101.49 % ±2.44 in case of tap, irrigation, and river water, respectively showing that there is no interference from the matrix in the samples with the determination of Fe3+. This result indicates that, the developed method had a great potential for quantification of Fe3
+ in samples of real water with high selectivity and accuracy.

The employment of low time and low energy-consuming synthesis processes along with safe renewable and cheap sources of carbon makes the developed method's greenness, cost, and safety effect obvious. Among the most up-to-date software for assessing the environmental friendliness of analytical processes in this context is the ComplexGAPI (Complementary Green Analytical Process Index) [85]. It serves as a comprehension assessment of the entire analytical process starting from obtaining sample to final analysis, which involves transportation, sample preparation, storage, and preservation. The scale is based on using 5 pentagrams relate to the eco-friendly features of the process, involving the chemicals, procedures, and devices with consideration for a number of factors like the amount, health and safety hazards from chemicals, waste generation, energy use, and method type (qualitative/quantitative), and the added hexagonal part evaluates the pre-analysis step which involves assessing the environment, equipment, reagents solvents, work up, occupational hazard and final product. For each segment of the ComplexGAPI pictogram, the green color indicates an environmentally favorable step, yellow represents moderate environmental impact, and red indicates a dangerous environmental impact. The pictogram displayed in Fig. S3a illustrates the obtained result and show the green feature of the developed method. The developed approach's greenness was further assessed using the Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) [86] which examines sample processing, the quantity and toxicity of solvents used, the energy used, and the waste generated. According to Fig. S3b, the overall score is 0.73, ensuring the greenness of the method.

Validation of the greeness of the suggested method by the two greenness measurements shows that the developed method has exceptional green features as the synthesis of CQDs fluorescent probes can be done in a single pot within a short time with very little energy used as compared to the reported hydrothermal methods [60,62–64,66,68,69]. The developed method is environmentally safe because there is no hazardous chemicals or solvents used as well as this method is affordable since it depends on utilising inexpensive and available substrates by using home microwave. Besides the recycling of plant wastes such as cauliflower leaves and stalk is very beneficial not only for protecting the environmental from bad impact of these wastes but also in making value from valueless substances, that supports research and development in limited resource laboratories and cooperates the sustainability and circular economy practice. All of these factors support the developed analytical technique's greenness and encourage the use of it for determining Fe3+ and for water analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of the reported and the new developed approach for Fe3+ determination
ce:section.ce:para:
Numerous benefits are revealed when the result of the CQDs to determine Fe3
+ was compared to the published methods as shown in Table S3 [25–30,87–91]. Greenness [92] is the most outstanding feature of the developed method. In this approach, the greenness starting from the carbon source till the synthesis process to produce safe and eco-friendly CQDs without using chemicals and organic solvents unlike many published Fe3+ determination procedures [25–30] that used organic solvents or chemicals including acetonitrile, nitric acid, ammonia, and hydrogen peroxide. For instance, the documented electrochemical method for Fe3+ determination needs time to prepare electrodes and use chemicals [28], and the HPLC approach [29] is time-consuming and organic solvents is used in this approach such as acetonitrile in contrast to the proposed method which requires less time and omits organic solvent. CQDs in the reported spectrofluorimetric methods prepared by hydrothermal method necessitate very long time and require high temperature [30,87,88]. Additionally, specific chemicals such as ionic liquids, 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide, glycolic acid, L-arginine, ethanol, n-heptane are needed [90,91]. Concerning LOD and sensitivity, the reported methods such as spectrophotometry [25,26], spectrofluorimetry [87–89], and ion chromatography [27] are less sensitive than the suggested method. Besides, the reported spectrofluorimetric techniques based on CQDs [88–90] have lower quantum yield (10.85 and 26.7 %) in comparison to the developed CQDs which have high quantum yield (42 %).

Accordingly, the new approach has notable benefits over many published methods for Fe3
+ determination. This supports the suggested procedure to determine Fe3
+ in terms of greenness, safety, sensitivity, rapidness, conditions, and quantum yield.

This study demonstrates the synthesis of CQDs from natural substrates; garlic and cauliflower by-products, which are easily found in nearby markets. The new approach supports sustainability goals and helps implanting circular economy practice in chemistry laboratories. The one-pot and one-step synthesis using a household microwave allowed for the quick production of CQDs in about 8 min to generate a simple, sensitive, quick, affordable, ecofriendly, and efficient technique for the creation of multi-doped CQDs. The CQDs were completely characterized by various methods indicating self-doping with sulfur, phosphorus, nitrogen, and minor elements like sodium and potassium, and inclusion of surface O, S, P, and N-containing function groups. The CQDs display high fluorescence quantum yield of 42 % and high stability for more than 6 months that allowed their application as fluorescence optosensor for environmental and pharmaceutical analysis of Fe3
+ over the concentration range of 10.0–400.0 μM. The CQDs have been used for quantitative determination of Fe3+ in pharmaceutical ampoules with percentage recovery ± SD of 98.73 ± 0.75 %. In addition, the prepared CQDs were applied for water analysis in tap, irrigation, and river water with excellent %recovery (99.45 ± 1.71, 100.57 ± 1.32, and 101.49 ± 2.44 %, respectively). It is envisioned that the CQDs-based optosensor would be widely applied for sensing Fe3+ in the future.
